questions.questionText: A 20-year-old has well-defined, chalk-white depigmented patches with no change in texture, on the dorsum of hands and around the mouth. Wood's lamp shows bright fluorescence of the patches. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Vitiligo (autoimmune destruction of melanocytes — well-defined depigmented macules, enhanced by Wood's lamp, Koebner positive)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Vitiligo = well-defined, chalk-white depigmented patches (no melanocytes). Why correct: Vitiligo: autoimmune melanocyte destruction. Well-defined borders, normal texture, bilateral/symmetrical, commonly on face (perioral, periorbital), acral areas (hands, feet), genitalia. Wood's lamp: bright white fluorescence (complete depigmentation). Koebner positive. Why A wrong: Pityriasis alba — ILL-DEFINED, scaly, HYPO-pigmented (not depigmented) patches, children, atopic. Why C wrong: Tinea versicolor — scaly hypo/hyperpigmented patches, KOH positive (Malassezia). Why D wrong: Leprosy — hypopigmented patches WITH anesthesia (loss of sensation) + nerve thickening. Clinical relevance: Types: generalized (most common), segmental (unilateral), acrofacial. Associations: autoimmune thyroiditis, DM, Addison's. Treatment: topical steroids/tacrolimus, NB-UVB phototherapy. Exam trap: Vitiligo = depigmented (NO melanocytes); pityriasis alba = HYPO-pigmented (reduced melanin). Wood's lamp differentiates. Memory aid: 'Vitiligo = Very white (depigmented), Well-defined, autoimmune.' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Pigmentary.
